title: Addressable Rydberg excitation in arrays of single neutral atoms with a strongly focused flat-top beam
authors: I. V. Iukhnovets, M. Y. Goloshchapov, A. P. Gordeev, O. V. Bychkova, I. B. Bobrov, G. I. Struchalin, S. S. Straupe
abstract: We present a method for generating a laser beam with flat intensity and phase profiles in the focal region where the beam interacts with neutral $^{87}$Rb atoms in an array of optical dipole traps. We synthesize the beam as a superposition of Hermite--Gaussian or Laguerre--Gaussian modes. Then we give analytical expressions for the coefficients of such a superposition, an analysis of beam propagation along the $z$ axis in the vicinity of the waist, and several other related theoretical issues. Rydberg two-qubit dynamics driven by this flat-top profile are analyzed through numerical solutions of the Lindblad master equation using our in-house Julia package. Beam preparation is demonstrated on a neutral-atom experimental platform. Measurements reveal a difference in the visibility of Rabi oscillations for addressed atoms compared with neighboring ones, confirming the effective spatial selectivity provided by the flat-top beam profile.
summary: This paper presents a method to create 'flat-top' laser beams for precise, site-selective Rydberg excitation in neutral-atom arrays. By superimposing Hermite-Gaussian or Laguerre-Gaussian modes using a spatial light modulator, the authors achieve highly uniform intensity profiles. This technique minimizes crosstalk between neighboring atoms and ensures consistent Rabi frequencies across the target array. The approach is experimentally validated, showing high uniformity and providing a scalable path for high-fidelity quantum gates.
main_problem: The need for high-fidelity, site-selective Rydberg excitation in neutral-atom arrays by overcoming the non-uniformity of laser beam intensity and phase profiles that cause crosstalk and gate errors.
main_result: The development of a method to synthesize strongly focused 'flat-top' beams using a superposition of Hermite-Gaussian or Laguerre-Gaussian modes, demonstrating intensity uniformity exceeding 99% and significant crosstalk suppression.
method: Beam shaping is achieved via a phase-only Spatial Light Modulator (SLM) to imprint a hologram, while the dynamics are analyzed using numerical solutions of the Lindblad master equation and Monte Carlo wave function methods.
model_or_system: The system consists of arrays of single $^{87}$Rb atoms trapped in optical dipole traps, utilizing a two-photon Rydberg excitation scheme ($5S_{1/2} 	o 5P_{1/2} 	o 60S_{1/2}$) and the Rydberg blockade effect.
key_observables: Rabi oscillation visibility, atomic ionization density maps, crosstalk parameter (ratio of Rabi frequencies), and atom survival probability.
important_parameters: Rabi frequency ($\approx 2.4$ MHz), atom temperature ($\approx 100$ $\mu$K), beam waist ($w_x \approx 1.1$ $\mu$m, $w_y \approx 3.0$ $\mu$m), and trap depth ($800$ $\mu$K).
main_assumptions: The paraxial approximation is used for beam propagation, and the beam phase is assumed to be uniform in the flat region.
figures_summary: Schematics of the excitation process and experimental setup; illustrations of the flat-top profile zones; plots of SLM holograms with and without aberration correction; and data on Rabi oscillations and the error budget for CZ gates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of beam non-uniformity, provides a theoretical framework for constructing flat-top beams via mode superposition, details the experimental implementation using SLM and aberration correction, and concludes with numerical/experimental validation of crosstalk suppression and error analysis.
why_interesting: It provides a practical solution for improving gate fidelities in neutral-atom quantum computing by combining advanced optical beam-shaping theory with experimental implementation and rigorous error budgeting.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illumination of atoms in a trap array by two Rydberg-excitation beams. (a) Scheme of two-photon exci- tation to the |r⟩state via the intermediate |p⟩state. (b) Experimental realization. The beam for the first excitation stage is global and propagates along the OX direction. The for the second excitation stage provides site-selective address- ing, propagates along OZ, and has a flat-top intensity profile.
FIG. 2. Flat-top profile zones (the color scale represents the intensity normalized to its maximum value)
FIG. 3. (a) Flat-top beam constructed as a superposition of the first five even Hermite–Gaussian modes. (b) Dependence of the flat-top width and intensity on N.

FIG. 4. Longitudinal intensity and phase profiles for a Gaussian beam (a, b) and the flat-top beam of x- and y-axis orders N = M = 8 (c, d). Phase profiles do not include the factor exp(−ikz). Intensities are normalized to their respective peak values; phases are given in radians. The intensity panels also include the analytical Gaussian-beam waist for reference (gray lines).
FIG. 5. Representative examples of holograms generated for flat-top beam preparation using an SLM. (a) Without aber- ration correction. (b) Superimposed with the hologram for aberration correction.
FIG. 6. Effective 5-level system. (a) Full hyperfine structure of 52S1/2 and 52P1/2. (b) Reduced atomic levels with effective decay level |L⟩.

FIG. 7. (a) Optical scheme of the experimental setup. (b) Computational zone (shown centered; when forming the computa- tional array, either the five leftmost or the five rightmost columns are used) and storage zone.

FIG. 8. (a) Atom ionization density map. The addressed atoms are shown in blue and green. Other colors correspond to the non-addressed atoms. (b) Transverse profile of the beam along the x-axis approximated by Gaussian. (c) Transverse profile of the beam along the y-axis approximated by a flat-top profile.
FIG. 9. Modeling and experimental results. (a) Rydberg Rabi oscillations on addressed atoms. (b) Rydberg Rabi oscillations on nonaddressed atoms. (c) CZ gate error budget.
FIG. 10. The atom temperature is extracted by fitting the release–recapture curve with a Monte Carlo simulation.
